translation: Woe to every scorner and mocker
commentary:
وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ

The connection between Surah Al-Asr and Surah Al-Humazah is remarkable. While Surah Al-Asr establishes that every human being is in loss, Al-Humazah takes this concept further by describing those who are in the worst kind of loss. This surah begins and ends with descriptions of hell, making it clear that we are dealing with the gravest of spiritual dangers.

The word (وَيْلٌ) carries tremendous weight. It represents a curse so severe, so beyond ordinary description, that the Arabic language leaves it undefined and mysterious - indicated by the tanwin (nunation). Some scholars describe wayl as a specific valley in hell so terrible that even hell itself is said to fear it. Others understand it as an expression of extreme frustration and anger. In the Quran, Allah ﷻ uses this word to convey the highest degree of divine displeasure.

The two qualities described here - humazah and lumazah - are not two different types of people, but rather two manifestations of the same diseased personality. The construction "humazatin lumazah" (without a connecting "wa" between them) tells us these are two sides of the same coin.

The dominant understanding among scholars is that humazah refers to someone who attacks others behind their backs, while lumazah describes someone who insults people to their faces. Imam Ibn Kathir notes that humazah can include subtle forms of mockery: the way someone looks at you, their facial expressions, a dismissive gesture, or the contempt visible in their eyes. Lumazah, on the other hand, involves direct verbal attacks and disparaging remarks.

Umm Al-Mumineen Aisha (RA) used a beautiful methodology to understand these words - she looked at how Allah ﷻ uses them elsewhere in the Quran. When the Quran mentions "hamazat ash-shayatin" (the whisperings of the devils), it refers to secret whispers, suggesting humazah is about speaking behind someone's back. When hypocrites openly criticized the Prophet ﷺ about the distribution of charity, the word "yalmizuka" (from lamz) was used, indicating lumazah refers to open criticism.

The root of humazah comes from the imagery of a powerful bow pulled tight. When it releases an arrow, it penetrates its target precisely and painfully at the point of impact. This is someone who knows exactly what to say to wound another person, who targets their vulnerabilities with precision, stabbing at their dignity and self-worth.

Now we must ask: why does Allah ﷻ begin by condemning this behavior rather than shirk, murder or theft? Because the dignity of the believer is sacred to Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ once stood before the Kaaba during tawaf and said, "How pure you are, how good your scent is, how grand your sanctity. By the One who has Muhammad's life in His hands, the dignity of the believer is more sacred to Allah than your dignity." He was pointing at the Kaaba - that house whose liberation was the entire mission of his prophethood - and declaring that a believer's honor is more sacred to Allah ﷻ than even the Kaaba.

The Prophet ﷺ continued: the believer's wealth, blood, and honor are sacred. Then he said something profound about the third element: "We should think nothing but good about him." Even our thoughts about other believers must be guarded. This is why believers are described in the Quran as "humble before one another" (adhillah) - when you are truly humble before someone, you cannot think of criticizing them. You think only of your own shortcomings.

Consider also that when Aisha's (RA) honor was attacked through slander, Allah ﷻ revealed Quranic verses to defend her. She later reflected that she never thought herself worthy of having Allah's ﷻ own words revealed about her situation - perhaps a dream shown to the Prophet ﷺ, but not Quran. Yet to protect her dignity, Allah ﷻ sent the higher form of revelation. Meanwhile, to restore the Kaaba to Muslim hands, He sent the Prophet ﷺ a dream. The protection of a believer's dignity warranted Quran; the liberation of the Kaaba warranted a vision. This shows us the weight Allah ﷻ places on our treatment of one another.

When the Quran describes someone committing shirk or saying something blasphemous about Allah ﷻ, it instructs us to respond with "Subhan Allah." But when believers heard slander about Aisha (RA), the Quran asked: "Why didn't you say 'Subhanaka' (You are perfect, O Allah) when you heard it?" The same term used for blasphemy against Allah ﷻ was used for an attack on a believer's honor. The conversation should have stopped immediately with recognition that this is an offense against Allah ﷻ Himself.

This helps us understand why wayl - why such severe anger - is directed at these people. When we hurt another believer, we commit a crime not merely against that person, but against Allah ﷻ who honored them, who created them, who declared their dignity sacred.

Why do people become consumed with pointing out others' flaws? Deep insecurity and lack of self-respect drive this behavior. When you have no respect for yourself, when you see no dignity in your own character, the only way to feel superior is to push others down. You cannot elevate yourself, so you dedicate yourself to lowering others. This is actually a manifestation of arrogance - kibr. The Prophet ﷺ warned that no one with even an atom's weight of arrogance will enter Paradise. Arrogance means seeing yourself as better than others, and constantly criticizing others is precisely how that arrogance manifests.

translation: Who collects wealth and [continuously] counts it
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًا وَعَدَّدَهُۥ

This ayah now describes another dimension of this diseased personality. After telling us about their treatment of others, Allah ﷻ reveals their relationship with wealth. The relative pronoun "alladhi" (the one who) connects this directly to the previous description - this is the same person who demeans others.

"Jama'a malan" - he gathered wealth. The verb implies constant accumulation, hoarding, bringing it all together for himself. Then comes "addadahu" - he counted it repeatedly. The word 'addada implies not just counting once, but counting again and again and again, obsessively verifying it, reviewing it, making sure it's all there.

But 'addada carries another meaning: he made others count it too. He ensured people knew about his wealth. At parties, in conversations, he finds ways to mention his new car, his property, his assets. "I can't believe I just got the new model - I don't even have room in my garage anymore!" This isn't just about having wealth; it's about making sure everyone knows you have it, making sure it gets counted, tallied, noticed, envied.

Some people have essentially become walking advertisements for brands. They're not individuals with character and substance - they're coat hangers for labels, living billboards announcing their possessions. This obsession with material display is compensation for a void inside that remains unfilled no matter how much they acquire or show off.

Why this obsession with wealth? Because they've determined their worth by their possessions rather than the content of their character. They no longer see themselves as having intrinsic value that Allah ﷻ gave them. Instead, they need external validation through material things. The more they have, the more they can display, the more worthy they believe they are.

Our culture constantly reinforces this lie - that your worth equals your financial worth. If you don't have an impressive degree, a high-status job, the right house, the right car, then who are you to speak? What do you have? This message gets internalized so deeply that even poor people begin to think this way. When they finally get some money, they immediately spend it on something to display rather than on what they actually need, because showing worth has become more important than genuine wellbeing.

But Allah ﷻ doesn't measure human worth this way. A person sleeping under the stars in a desert, hungry for days, may be infinitely more dignified to Allah ﷻ than a billionaire. Yet the billionaire - and those dazzled by wealth - can become so deluded by the false image they've created that they truly believe their money makes them superior. This is what the Quran calls "mukhtaal" - someone on a high horse, having a lofty opinion of themselves that exists only in their imagination.

translation: He thinks that his wealth will make him immortal
commentary:
يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ

"Yahsabu" means he assumes, he thinks, he calculates. Notice the irony - this word shares the same root as counting (hasaba), connecting back to how he counted his wealth. In his calculation, he has convinced himself of something absurd: that his money has made him eternal.

Look carefully at the verb tense. Allah ﷻ doesn't say "his money will make him eternal" (sa-yukhliduhu). He says "akhladahu" - his money has already made him eternal. It's in the past tense, suggesting it's already done, already accomplished. What does this mean?

It means he lives as though death isn't real, as though his current state will never change. He doesn't think about mortality. His mind goes from weekend to weekend, party to party, acquisition to acquisition. Between these moments, when thoughts about real purpose or meaning try to surface, he drowns them out with entertainment. He's drugged himself with materialism more effectively than any substance could do.

Or he tells himself that his money will immortalize him through legacy - buildings with his name, institutions bearing his family name, monuments that will make people remember him forever. The Quran addresses this elsewhere: "You make carvings and monuments hoping to live forever" - as if stone structures could grant eternity to your name or meaning to your existence.

This attitude was captured perfectly in Surah Al-Kahf with the man who looked at his gardens and said, "This will never go away" (lan tabida abada). He couldn't conceive of losing what he had. That's not just thinking you'll live a million years - it's acting like bad times will never come, like you're permanently set, like nothing could ever change your situation. It's the ultimate delusion of those intoxicated by wealth.

translation: No! He will surely be thrown into the Crusher
commentary:
كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ

"Kalla!" - Absolutely not! No! This is a strong, emphatic negation. You've got it completely wrong, and now let me tell you what's really going to happen.

"Layunbadhanna" - he will absolutely, certainly be cast away. The verb "nabdh" is what you do with worthless trash. When you're sitting in your car eating chocolate and there are crumbs and wrappers on the seat - you brush them off without thought. When there's a dirty napkin or garbage on the ground, you sweep it away. That's nabdh - disposing of something that has no value, that's beneath consideration.

Allah ﷻ is saying: this person who thought they were worth so much, who made others feel worthless, who believed their wealth gave them ultimate value - they will be thrown away like garbage. You thought you were something? You're just trash.

"Fil-hutamah" - into the Crusher. The word "hutamah" comes from "hatm," which refers to tiny fragments of dried leaves that people have stepped on until they've disintegrated into barely visible flakes. When you walk on hutam, do you feel like you're stepping on something valuable? Do you carefully avoid it? No - it's by definition meant to be stepped on. It's worthless.

Allah ﷻ describes this person's destination not just as hellfire, but specifically as the place that crushes worthless things. Both the verb (cast like trash) and the noun (the crusher) emphasize their utter worthlessness. Why? Because all they did in the first two ayahs was establish their false sense of worth - through putting others down and through wealth accumulation. Now Allah ﷻ strips away that delusion completely.

The word hutamah appears elsewhere in the Quran. The ants in Surah An-Naml feared being crushed (yahtimannakum). The term is used for dried, peeled-off layers - like the skin of a boiled egg that's discarded. Camels are called "hutumah" because they crush whatever is in front of them as they walk. Now Allah ﷻ uses this word for a portion of hell.

translation: And what can make you know what is the Crusher
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ

"And what will make you understand what the Crusher is?" This interrogative style increases the mystery and horror. The word "hutamah" was unfamiliar to the Arabs as a name for hell - they might have thought it referred to crushing animals like camels. So Allah ﷻ poses this question to create anticipation and dread. What could this crusher be?

translation: It is the fire of Allah, [eternally] fueled
commentary:
نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ

The answer:  the fire of Allah, the one that has been kindled. This is the only place in the entire Quran where Allah ﷻ attributes fire directly to Himself with this possessive construction. Not just "fire" but specifically "Allah's fire." Do you grasp how angry Allah ﷻ is? Do you understand that this crime - though it seems to be against people - is actually being committed against Allah ﷻ Himself?

"Al-muqadah" - the one that has been lit and continues to be fueled. The verb "awqada" means to fuel a fire. The form "muqadah" (passive participle) means fire that is continually, perpetually being fueled. It never runs out of fuel. It keeps burning and burning and burning.

Notice the painful irony: this person's greed was endless. They kept gathering and gathering, accumulating and displaying, never satisfied, always wanting more. Their heart was filled with limitless desire for wealth. So Allah ﷻ gives them a fire with limitless fuel. That heart that was consumed by endless greed will now be consumed by endless flames. The punishment mirrors the crime.

translation: Which mounts directed at the hearts
commentary:
ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ

which climbs up upon the hearts. The verb "tattali'u" means to climb upward. Think about this: if they're in the Crusher being crushed, where are they positioned? At the bottom. So we expect punishment from above, pressing down. But Allah ﷻ says the fire climbs up to their hearts - meaning it also comes from below.

They're trapped between two directional punishments. From above, fire crushes them. From below, fire crawls up toward their hearts like a snake or insect climbing their body, penetrating into the core of their being.

Why specifically the hearts? Because they hurt other people's hearts. Because they carried love of wealth in their hearts where love of Allah ﷻ should have been. The heart was the real criminal. Yes, the tongue spoke the hurtful words. Yes, the eyes gave the contemptuous looks. Yes, the hands made the insulting gestures. But all of these external manifestations came from a corrupted heart. So the punishment goes to the source - the heart that was the root of all these crimes.

The word used here is "af'idah" (plural of fu'ad). While "qalb" is the general word for heart, "fu'ad" specifically refers to a heart that is intensely engaged, overwhelmed with strong emotion - anger, fear, love, extreme sadness. When your heart is in an intense, burning state, it becomes fu'ad. Allah ﷻ chooses this word because the heart will be literally and figuratively on fire.

There's a profound figurative reading here as well. Every person you hurt, every heart you wounded, every individual whose dignity you trampled - you didn't feel their pain when you did it. Had you felt it, had you truly understood what you were doing, you wouldn't have done it. But on that Day, you will feel it. All the pain you caused will combine into a concentrated flame inside your heart. You will finally feel what they felt, and you'll feel it eternally. This is the fire climbing into your heart - the accumulated agony you inflicted on others, now turned back upon you.

translation: Indeed, Hellfire will be closed down upon them
commentary:
إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ

indeed, it is closed tight upon them. The word "mu'sadah" comes from "asada," which means to shut something completely, to seal it airtight. Think of a door locked so tight nothing can get through, or a container sealed so perfectly that it's vacuum-packed.

This fire that's crushing them from above and climbing up from below - it's also hermetically sealed around them. There's no gap, no opening, no room to move, no way to escape. It's claustrophobic, suffocating, absolutely airless.

Why this specific punishment? Remember the principle in Islamic theology: Allah's ﷻ punishments are precisely calibrated to the crimes committed. They suffocated others. They took wealth that rightfully belonged to others - family members who deserved support, employees who deserved fair wages, people who deserved compassion - and kept it all for themselves, creating tightness and difficulty in other people's lives.

Think about workers in some countries forced to labor 20 hours a day for minimal wages. If they get sick, if they're late even five minutes, they're kicked out, sent back to poverty with no recourse. Think about five or six cab drivers sharing one mattress in a single room, taking turns sleeping in four-hour shifts between their exhausting work schedules, sending whatever they can back to starving families. Meanwhile, the people profiting from their labor have wealth beyond imagination - wealth they couldn't spend in a hundred lifetimes - but they refuse to pay fair wages or provide humane conditions.

A very small percentage of people control the vast majority of the world's wealth, and because of this hoarding, the majority of humanity suffocates economically. If those who hoard let go of even half their wealth, the world would transform. But they can't let go because their identity is wrapped up in accumulation. They created suffocation for others, so now it's tightened upon them - "mu'sadah."

فِى عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ

"Fi 'amadin mumaddadah" - in columns extended endlessly. The word "'amad" (plural of 'amud) refers to pillars, the structural columns that hold up a building and bear the most weight.

There are two ways to understand this. The straightforward reading: they are trapped inside these pillars, or underneath them, being crushed by enormous weight. The heaviest part of any structure is the pillar itself - imagine being trapped under that, with all that pressure bearing down on you.

"Mumaddadah" means stretched out, extended. But Allah ﷻ didn't use the simple form "mamdudah" (stretched). He used the intensive form "mumaddadah" - stretched beyond stretched, extended beyond extension, endless. These columns keep stretching upward perpetually, just as their greed stretched perpetually. The weight keeps increasing, the crushing continues without end.

Notice the beautiful - and terrifying - linguistic parallel. In ayah 2, the person "addadahu" (counted it repeatedly). Here the columns are "mumaddadah" (extended repeatedly). The pattern mirrors the crime. They endlessly counted their wealth; now they're under endlessly extending columns.

Another possible reading: they're in a prison cell, and when they look at the bars, they see columns stretching upward infinitely, reinforcing their eternal imprisonment. The word "'amad" also means something that doesn't go away - it's permanent, immovable, fixed. From this root we get "a'mada" - when you're firmly determined about something. The columns represent permanence, and for these people, that permanence means eternal punishment.

## Reflection on the Surah's Structure

The structure of this surah is remarkable. At its center (ayah 4) stands the crushing - "kalla layunbadhanna fil-hutamah" - ultimate destruction, complete worthlessness. If something is crushed into hutam (tiny fragments), you cannot be more lost than that. This is the worst possible end.

Before this central declaration come three crimes:
1. Hurting people through condescension and mockery (humazatin lumazah)
2. Obsession with accumulating and displaying wealth (jama'a malan wa-'addadahu)
3. Living as though wealth grants eternity (yahsabu anna malahu akhladahu)

After the central declaration come three punishments:
1. Allah's ﷻ fire that climbs up to the hearts (narullahi al-muqadah allati tattali'u 'ala al-af'idah)
2. Sealed tight upon them (innaha 'alayhim mu'sadah)
3. In columns extended endlessly (fi 'amadin mumaddadah)

Each punishment corresponds precisely to its crime:
- They hurt others' hearts → Fire reaches their hearts
- They obsessed over expansion and accumulation → They're compressed and sealed tight
- They thought wealth gave them endless life → They're under endlessly extending columns

Even the vocabulary shows this correspondence:
- Humazah/Lumazah parallel Hutamah (same morphological pattern)
- 'Addada (counted repeatedly) parallels Mumaddadah (extended repeatedly)

The middle section (ayahs 5-6) stands distinct: "What will make you understand what the Crusher is? Allah's ﷻ fire that has been kindled." These two elements bind the crimes to their punishments, showing that this is Allah's ﷻ direct judgment.

## Final Reflection

This surah doesn't require that someone be a billionaire to be guilty of these crimes. You can have little and still be obsessed with it. You can show off within your small circle. The disease isn't the amount; it's what's in the heart. Once love of wealth and obsession with status penetrate your heart, once putting others down becomes your way of feeling worthy, you've entered dangerous territory.

The surah leaves these descriptions open-ended. "Waylun likulli humazatin lumazah" - woe to every, to each, to any person with these qualities. It could be anyone. It could be you. It could be me. That's why we must search our hearts, identify these diseases within ourselves, and work to remove them.

If you're suffering at the hands of people like this - people who demean you, who use wealth to make you feel inferior, who crush your dignity - know that Allah ﷻ sees. He sees your pain, and He will hold them accountable with perfect justice. Your dignity is sacred to Him, more sacred than the Kaaba itself.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from becoming people who deserve to be crushed. May He purify our hearts of arrogance, greed and contempt for others. May He help us recognize the sacred dignity He placed in every believer, and may He make us among those who honor others as He has commanded. May we recite these ayahs not with mere tongues, but with hearts that truly understand their weight and live by their guidance.
